planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 3–4 weeks before) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 12–18 inches and sidedress with nitrogen at 30 days for best grain production.

directions: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 3–4 weeks before) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 12–18 inches and sidedress with nitrogen at 30 days for best grain production. Amaranthus cruentus is a remarkable dual-purpose annual producing enormous, showy, red-purple to magenta flower plumes from midsummer through fall while simultaneously growing highly nutritious seeds (up to 20% protein with a complete amino acid profile including lysine) that can be harvested as a pseudo-grain and popped, ground into flour, or cooked like porridge; the young leaves and shoots are also edible as a nutritious cooking green high in iron and calcium. The bold, often dark-red to bronze-red foliage is highly ornamental; selected grain varieties include 'Burgundy', 'Hot Biscuits', and 'Hopi Red Dye'. Young leaves are an excellent spinach substitute in hot weather when true spinach bolts.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; amaranth is one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant grain crops available and requires little supplemental water once established.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; amaranth is one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant grain crops available and requires little supplemental water once established. Amaranthus hypochondriacus was one of the three staple crops of Aztec civilization (along with maize and beans), consumed as grain in porridge and flatbreads (tamales de alegría) and in ritual religious offerings to Huitzilopochtli; the Spanish conquistadors banned its cultivation as part of their campaign against Aztec religion, contributing to the decline of amaranth as a staple food. The grain is nutritionally exceptional — 14–18% protein with a balanced amino acid profile including lysine — and is now being rediscovered as an important gluten-free alternative grain. The dramatic tall stems with enormous, feathery, dark-red to magenta to gold plumes are visually spectacular in the garden and support abundant seed for birds in autumn.
sources: National Academy of Sciences (Lost Crops of the Incas)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost in warm (75–80°F), well-drained seed-starting mix, do not overwater; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm, spacing 9–12 inches in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun.

directions: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost in warm (75–80°F), well-drained seed-starting mix, do not overwater; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm, spacing 9–12 inches in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Celosia argentea encompasses the plumed celosia (var. plumosa) with feathery flame-shaped flower heads and the brain-like crested cockscomb (var. cristata), both of which come in brilliant, jewel-toned shades of red, orange, yellow, pink, and cream; the flower heads are outstanding dried everlastings and fresh cut flowers. In tropical and subtropical Africa, the leaves and young shoots are an important leafy vegetable (known as "soko" in Nigeria and "quélites" in parts of Mexico), eaten boiled or added to soups and stews; the plant is nutritious and fast-growing. Very heat-tolerant and long-blooming; an excellent zone 9–11 annual.
sources: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

planting.note: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface after last frost (seeds need light — do not cover) or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; portulaca demands dry, poor, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun and will rot in heavy, moist, fertile conditions — this is the annual for the hot, dry, rocky spots where nothing else will grow.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface after last frost (seeds need light — do not cover) or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; portulaca demands dry, poor, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun and will rot in heavy, moist, fertile conditions — this is the annual for the hot, dry, rocky spots where nothing else will grow. Portulaca grandiflora is a prostrate, succulent annual adapted to hot, dry, sandy conditions that makes a spectacular low groundcover with bright green, needle-like leaves and a profusion of large (1–2 inch), tissue-paper-textured, cup-shaped flowers in vivid shades of red, magenta, orange, yellow, and white; flowers are single-day bloomers that open in full sun and close at night or in cloudy weather. It is virtually indestructible in the heat, requires essentially no water once established, is perfect for hot pavement cracks, dry slopes, and rock gardens, and provides important nectar for specialist Portulaca-visiting native bees. Modern double-flowered varieties stay open longer through the day.

planting.note: Start from seed (surface sow, seeds need light) 10–12 weeks before last frost or purchase transplants; plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost; deadhead regularly to extend bloom and pinch back leggy stems to maintain a bushy habit.

directions: Start from seed (surface sow, seeds need light) 10–12 weeks before last frost or purchase transplants; plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost; deadhead regularly to extend bloom and pinch back leggy stems to maintain a bushy habit. Petunia integrifolia is one of the primary wild parent species of all modern hybrid petunias, a semi-trailing South American native with small (1–1.5 inch), violet-purple to rose-magenta funnel-shaped flowers on sticky, hairy stems; it is both more heat-tolerant and more attractive to bumblebees than most hybrid petunias (which have been bred with flowers too large for native bees to access efficiently). In zone 9–10 gardens it can be grown as a short-lived perennial and self-seeds modestly in suitable conditions; the species is being rediscovered as a more ecologically valuable alternative to hybrid petunia mixes for pollinator gardens.
